ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for broccoli mattar masala. Mapped to the broader curry food product class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — cooked vegetable dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for broccoli mattar masala. A0CKP (cooked vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Indian vegetable curry preparation. SNOMED CT food coding is limited for ethnic cuisine dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Broccoli Mattar Masala is approximately 250 g, comprising broccoli florets and green peas in a spiced tomato-onion masala sauce, served alongside rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Matar Paneer / Vegetable Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for broccoli (NDB 11090) and green peas (NDB 11304) for component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Broccoli Mattar Masala exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by adapting the ICMR-NIN entry for vegetable curry (matar-based) and cross-referencing USDA FoodData Central data for cooked broccoli and green peas. The masala sauce contribution (oil, onion, tomato, spices) is estimated from ICMR-NIN vegetable curry reference. Confidence is medium due to recipe variability across the 8 Singapore restaurants.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — green peas and vegetable curry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Broccoli Mattar Masala. GI estimated at 45 based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: green peas (GI ~48, low), cooked broccoli (GI ~10–15, very low), and Indian vegetable curry with tomato-onion masala base (GI ~40–50 range per published analogues). The combined dish GI is estimated at 45 (low category). GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 7.2 g carbohydrate per 100g × 250g serving) / 100 = 8.1 g. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from masala sauce and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Broccoli Mattar Masala contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (775 mg per restaurant serving), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and monitor portion size to keep sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from broccoli and green peas
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 45 and a glycemic load of approximately 8.1 per serving, Broccoli Mattar Masala is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal; the dietary fibre (2.9 g per 100 g) from broccoli and peas further moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and cardioprotective phytonutrients from broccoli
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Broccoli Mattar Masala is low in saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with broccoli providing sulforaphane and folate that support vascular health; this dish is generally favourable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared with minimal oil and served without high-fat accompaniments such as butter naan.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli Mattar Masala is a North Indian vegetable curry dish consisting of broccoli florets and green peas (mattar or matar) cooked in a spiced tomato-onion masala sauce. The sauce is typically prepared by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil, then adding tomatoes and a blend of ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, and garam masala, creating a golden, mildly spicy gravy. Broccoli and green peas are simmered in this sauce until tender, absorbing the aromatic spice flavours. The dish has a savoury, mildly spiced taste with earthy notes from the cumin and coriander, and a slightly sweet undertone from the green peas. The texture contrasts tender-crisp broccoli florets with soft peas in a thick, flavourful sauce. In Singapore, this dish is served across multiple North Indian restaurants and is priced between SGD 14.90 and SGD 17.90 per portion. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g) that is high in dietary fibre, vitamin C, folate, and iron, while being low in saturated fat and free of cholesterol. It carries a low estimated glycemic index of 45, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and halal, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements. It is typically served with steamed basmati rice, roti, or naan.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Broccoli Mattar Masala exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting ICMR-NIN vegetable curry data and USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked broccoli and green peas. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using green peas and vegetable curry analogues. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and multi-source, but recipe variability across 8 Singapore restaurants introduces uncertainty.
notes: Broccoli Mattar Masala is a North Indian vegetable curry not individually listed in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN vegetable curry reference data and USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked broccoli (NDB 11090) and boiled green peas (NDB 11304), weighted for a typical restaurant recipe composition. Sodium content reflects restaurant-style preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D are reported as 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; these are confirmed zero by ingredient composition. Cholesterol is 0.0 confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. The GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish has been published. Ontology codes are mapped to broader categories as no specific FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for this dish. Follow-up: a direct GI measurement study for Indian vegetable curries would improve GI confidence. Restaurant-specific nutritional analysis would improve macro accuracy.
